elements.sent-2411-004: Using embeddings from state-of-the-art large language models, I show that textual information in analyst reports explains 10.19\% of contemporaneous stock returns out-of-sample, a value that is economically more significant than quantitative forecasts.
elements.sent-2411-005: I then perform a Shapley value decomposition to assess how much each topic within the reports contributes to explaining stock returns.
elements.sent-2411-006: The results show that analysts' income statement analyses account for more than half of the reports' explanatory power.
elements.sent-2411-007: Expressing these findings in economic terms, I estimate that early acquisition of analysts' reports can yield significant profits.
elements.sent-2411-008: Analysts' information value peeks in the first week following earnings announcements, highlighting their vital role in interpreting new financial data.
elements.sent-2411-009: They need a story.'' \begin{flushright ― Daniel Kahneman \end{flushright.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Sell-side analysts play a crucial role in financial markets by processing information for investors.
elements.sent-2411-011: Their research reports, which include both quantitative forecasts and qualitative analyses, are widely used by investors in decision-making (\citeay{barber2001can ; \citeay{kong2021whose ).}}
elements.sent-2411-012: This raises a fundamental question: Do the analysts generate value for their clients, or are they merely peddling expensive noise? Quantifying the value of analyst information, especially that contained in written reports, has been a persistent challenge in finance literature due to the unstructured nature of textual data.
elements.sent-2411-013: While prior studies have demonstrated the incremental information value of report text, these analyses have predominantly centered on the sentiment dimension (\citeay{asquith2005information ; \citeay{huang2014evidence ; \citeay{huang2018analyst ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, analyst reports are meant to provide contextual information perceived as at least as important as the quantifiable outputs.
elements.sent-2411-015: For example, according to the annual survey of Institutional Investor magazine, investors consistently rank ``Written Reports'' as more valuable than earnings forecasts and stock recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-016: Yet, empirical evidence supporting this survey feedback is currently limited, and it remains unclear which specific component of analyst reports holds the most value for investors.
elements.sent-2411-017: The advent of large language models (LLMs) has significantly enhanced our ability to quantify textual meaning, capturing more granular and rich information than before.
elements.sent-2411-018: This advancement facilitates a systematic analysis of the comprehensive outputs from sell-side analysts.
elements.sent-2411-019: For example, consider the following two statements: ``Reported EPS of \$0.94 miss consensus of \$1.40 and our estimate of \$1.17.'' and ``Reported 1Q12 MS franchise sales are below our estimate, as sales were negatively impacted by unfavorable distribution channel dynamics.'' While both sentences express negative sentiment and pertain to the "earnings analysis" topic, the second provides additional contextual information that LLMs can interpret and analyze.
elements.sent-2411-020: I quantify the value of information in analyst reports in three general steps: analyst outputs representation, econometric modeling, and value decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-021: Analyst outputs include both quantitative information and qualitative information.
elements.sent-2411-022: The quantitative data consists of numerical forecasts from I/B/E/S, such as stock recommendations, earnings forecasts, and target prices.
elements.sent-2411-023: For structured representation of qualitative textual information, I extract contextualized representations of analyst report texts using state-of-the-art LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-024: Each report is mapped into a structured embedding space using the LLMs' transformer architecture, allowing these to capture both contextual information and the reasoning logic within the text (\citeay{chen2022expected ; \citeay{li2023dissecting ).}}
elements.sent-2411-025: I evaluate the information content of analyst reports using an R-squared measure to quantify how well the information set explains stock returns.
elements.sent-2411-026: A significant challenge in projecting stock returns onto analyst outputs is the risk of in-sample overfitting due to the high-dimensionality of text embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-027: To address this issue, I employ Ridge regression, which introduces a regularization term to penalize large coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-028: This regularization helps in handling the high-dimensional embeddings while preserving the model's ability to capture essential relationships between analyst outputs and stock returns.
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, I assess the explanatory power of both quantitative and qualitative measures for abnormal stock returns using out-of-sample R-squared values with a recursively expanding window.
elements.sent-2411-030: I compile a comprehensive corpus of analyst reports from the Mergent Investext database, merging these written reports with detailed analyst forecast summaries from the I/B/E/S database.
elements.sent-2411-031: The final sample comprises 122,252 analyst reports on S\&P 100 constituent firms spanning from 2000 to 2023.
elements.sent-2411-032: Using this sample, the textual information generates an out-of-sample $R^2$ of 10.19\% for three-day cumulative abnormal returns ($CAR$), surpassing the 9.01\% explained by numerical information.
elements.sent-2411-033: When both textual and numerical measures are combined, the explainable variation in $CAR$ increases to 12.28\%, which is significantly higher than using each type of information individually.
elements.sent-2411-034: Economically, a one standard deviation increase in report text favorableness raises the realized CAR by 120 basis points, compared to 100 basis points for forecast revisions.
elements.sent-2411-035: These findings remain robust across various LLM text representations, machine learning algorithms, and $CAR$ windows, highlighting the substantial and unique information content winthin analyst report text.
elements.sent-2411-036: The informativeness of analyst reports varies considerably depending on whether they are associated with forecast revisions or reiterations.
elements.sent-2411-037: My analysis reveals that both qualitative and quantitative information provide greater value to the financial market when linked to forecast revisions.
elements.sent-2411-038: Specifically, when analyst reports involve recommendation revisions, the combined use of numerical and textual data yields an out-of-sample $R^2$ of 22.63\%.
elements.sent-2411-039: In stark contrast, for reports with earnings forecast reiterations, the out-of-sample $R^2$ is not statistically significant, suggesting that such reports contribute minimal incremental information to the market.
elements.sent-2411-040: Since a notable amount of research emphasizes the role of analysts in information processing around corporate earnings announcements, e.g., \citet{livnat2012information , \citet{keskek2014analyst , \citet{kim2015management , \citet{lobo2017effect , and \citet{barron2017earnings , I examine the information content of analyst reports both within and beyond the earnings announcement window.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: This investigation reveals that the information content of analyst reports peaks in the first week following earnings announcements, supporting \citet{huang2018analyst 's perspective that timely reactions to corporate disclosures offer valuable information advantages to clients.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Using separately trained models on samples within and beyond earnings announcement windows, I find that the magnitude of the out-of-sample $R^2$ nearly doubles in the first week following earnings announcement dates compared to other periods.
elements.sent-2411-043: This significant increase underscores the incremental value that analysts add by interpreting and contextualizing earnings information for investors.
elements.sent-2411-044: To address potential concerns that analysts might simply be ``piggybacking'' on information disclosed during earnings conference calls, I incorporate controls for both the earnings surprise and the text embeddings of the latest earnings conference call transcripts into models.
elements.sent-2411-045: By doing so, I can isolate the unique information of analyst reports.
elements.sent-2411-046: The findings indicate that even after accounting for the information from conference calls, analyst reports contain distinct and valuable insights, highlighting the different information sets provided by the two sources.
elements.sent-2411-047: As raised in the review paper of \citet{bradshaw2017financial , identifying the qualitative attributes of analyst reports that investors find most salient remains an open question.}
elements.sent-2411-048: To quantify the importance of different content in analyst reports, I leverage the additive feature of text embeddings and design a Shapley value decomposition approach to fully attribute the explanatory power to 17 major topics discussed in analyst reports.
elements.sent-2411-049: The results show that over half of the explanatory power of the report, in terms of out-of-sample $R^2$, is attributed to Income Statement Analyses in analyst reports, particularly the interpretation of realized income numbers.
elements.sent-2411-050: This evidence again emphasizes the critical role analysts play in interpreting financial data and providing the stories and context needed for making investment decisions.
elements.sent-2411-051: To translate the statistic measure of R-squared into economic terms, I quantify the dollar value of analyst information to strategic investors using \citet{Kadan_Manela_Infoval 's framework, which builds on \citet{back2000imperfect imperfect competition insider trading model.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: The information value can be interpreted as the total expected profits for strategic investors either because some investors have early access to analyst views or because they conduct independent research and derive insights comparable to those of analysts.
elements.sent-2411-053: This multi-client framework provides a realistic foundation for analyzing the complex dynamics of information dissemination in analyst reports.
elements.sent-2411-054: Essentially, the measure is defined as the ratio of the explainable return variance—which quantifies the reduction in uncertainty attributable to analyst insights—to the price impact, representing the cost associated with trading on this information.
elements.sent-2411-055: The findings reveal significant value in analyst information.
elements.sent-2411-056: For an average S\&P 100 stock over a three-day window, strategic investors’ expected profits from early access to analyst reports are \$0.34 million from numerical information, \$0.38 million from textual information, and \$0.47 million from both combined.

elements.sent-2411-059: Notably, these values increase for large stocks, bold analysts, and reports released promptly following earnings announcements.
elements.sent-2411-060: This paper relates to three strands of literature.

elements.sent-2411-062: Prior research often relies on manually encoding textual information and sentiment analyses, which is labor-intensive and may overlook nuanced insights.

elements.sent-2411-077: The rest of the paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-078: Section \ref{method introduces methodology, measures of analyst information value, and interpretation.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Section \ref{empirical summarizes data construction and empirical results.. Section \ref{conclu concludes.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: In this section, I introduce the large language models used for topic classification and text embeddings, the information content estimation of out-of-sample $R^2$, the strategic information value estimation of analyst reports, and the decomposition metric used to evaluate the information content of specific topics.\footnote{The details of machine learning models used to explain contemporaneous stock returns are discussed in the Online Appendix C.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Large Language Models such as Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT), Generative Pre-trained Transformer (GPT), and Large Language Model Meta AI (LLaMA) are trending in literature for understanding textual data.
elements.sent-2411-082: These models represent a paradigm shift in NLP, offering capabilities that go beyond traditional methods like word-to-vec (W2V) or Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic models.
elements.sent-2411-083: In this study, I harness the power of these advanced generative AI models to conduct a comprehensive analysis of analyst reports.
elements.sent-2411-084: The methodology employs a multi-faceted approach: fine-tuned BERT models for precise sentence classification based on predefined criteria, ChatGPT prompting for extracting and synthesizing major topics discussed by analysts, and LLaMA embedding for representing entire reports as structured high-dimensional vectors.
elements.sent-2411-085: This approach not only enriches information extraction but also provides a nuanced understanding of subtle linguistic cues and thematic elements that may influence market reactions.
elements.sent-2411-086: To effectively categorize the topics discussed in analyst reports, I employ ChatGPT prompting to extract exclusive and consistent topics and avoid ad-hoc classification.
elements.sent-2411-087: By leveraging AI, my goal is to identify the primary topics covered in these reports and assign each sentence to a single, specific topic category.
elements.sent-2411-088: This approach ensures a systematic and coherent classification of the content within analyst reports.
elements.sent-2411-089: In the first step, to derive manageable and meaningful topics, I input a random sample of 100 analyst reports into ChatGPT, requesting an analysis of the content to identify high-level categories.
elements.sent-2411-090: What are high-level mutually exclusive topics covered in these reports? Make sure that each sentence from the text file can be assigned to one of the topics.
elements.sent-2411-092: Additionally, a category labeled ``None of the Above'' is included to accommodate sentences that do not fit into any of the predefined topics.
elements.sent-2411-093: In total, this process generates 17 distinct topics for classification.
elements.sent-2411-094: The distribution of topics is relatively stable across years, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:stack .}
elements.sent-2411-095: To provide a visual representation of the content within each topic, Figure \ref{fig: a1 in the Online Appendix presents word clouds for each category.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Furthermore, Table \ref{tab: topic_example offers illustrative sentences extracted directly from analyst reports, providing concrete examples of the discussions typical to each category.}
elements.sent-2411-097: In the second step, a systematic approach is required to assign topic classifications to a large corpus of 6,975,114 sentences.
elements.sent-2411-098: To begin with, a random sample of 17,028 sentences from 100 analyst reports is selected from analyst reports.
elements.sent-2411-099: I employ the GPT-4-Turbo model through the ChatGPT API to categorize each sentence in this sample into a single, relevant topic.
elements.sent-2411-100: Determine which category of information it belongs to in the following 17 categories: \{categories\}.
elements.sent-2411-101: Pay attention to the sentence in the context of the report.
elements.sent-2411-102: Output your response in JSON format.

elements.sent-2411-104: The fine-tuned model demonstrates high reliability, achieving an accuracy score of 89\% on a testing sample.
elements.sent-2411-105: Income Statement Analyses and Financial Ratios are the most frequently discussed topics in analyst reports, accounting for 17.23\% and 15.65\% of all sentences, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-106: These are followed by Risk Factors (8.32\%), Valuation (8.10\%), and Investment Thesis (7.87\%), which collectively form the next tier of frequently discussed topics.
elements.sent-2411-107: Conversely, Appendices and Disclosures (0.50\%), Executive Summary (0.48\%), and Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) issues (0.23\%) emerge as the least prevalent topics.
elements.sent-2411-108: Several factors contribute to these observed frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-109: The low occurrence of Appendices and Disclosures can be attributed to pre-boilerplate cleaning procedures implemented in the data preparation phase.
elements.sent-2411-110: The scarcity of Executive Summary content may result from the difficulty of identifying this category at the sentence level.
elements.sent-2411-111: Notably, ESG-related content, while minimal overall, appears mainly after 2020, reflecting the recent rise in focus on these issues.
elements.sent-2411-112: Unlike traditional NLP methods, the LLM framework begins with a contextual representation of tokenized text, a process that involves several key steps.
elements.sent-2411-113: Prior to tokenization, the text undergoes normalization, which may include converting to lowercase, removing punctuation, and handling special characters.
elements.sent-2411-114: Depending on the strategy, tokenization involves breaking down text into smaller units, which can be words, subwords, or even characters.
elements.sent-2411-115: Within LLMs, tokens are mapped to unique identifiers (IDs) based on a predefined vocabulary.
elements.sent-2411-116: This vocabulary is learned during the model's training phase and contains all the tokens the model can understand.\footnote{BERT uses 30,000 tokens, OpenAI GPT models use around 50,257 tokens, and LLaMA models use approximately 32,000 tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-117: To illustrate this process, consider the following sentence from an analyst report on NVIDIA: ``We expect to see more GPU/SmartNIC integration as next-gen workloads grow and CPUs become a bigger bottleneck in the data center." The LLaMA tokenizer breaks this into 34 sub-word tokens, including ```We', `expect', `to', `see', `more', `GPU', `/', `Sm', `art', `N', `IC', `integration', `as', `next', `-', `gen', `work', `loads', `grow', `and', `CPU', `s', `become', `a', `bigger', `bott', `l', `ene', `ck', `in', `the', `data', `center', `.'".
elements.sent-2411-118: This granular approach allows for nuanced interpretation of complex terms.
elements.sent-2411-119: For instance, `next-gen workloads' becomes five separate tokens: `next', `-', `gen', `work', and `loads'.
elements.sent-2411-120: At the heart of LLMs lies the transformer architecture.
elements.sent-2411-121: Introduced by \citet{vaswani2017attention , the transformer architecture revolutionized the field of NLP with its novel approach to sequence-to-sequence tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Unlike recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and long short-term memory networks (LSTMs), transformers use a self-attention mechanism that processes input sequences in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-123: This approach allows the model to weigh the importance of different words in relation to each other, effectively capturing context regardless of word distance.
elements.sent-2411-124: The transformer's encoder-decoder structure, combined with multi-head self-attention and feed-forward networks, enables efficient processing of large datasets and state-of-the-art performance in various NLP tasks.
elements.sent-2411-125: Text embedding, a core output of the transformer architecture, transforms language into a format suitable for computational analysis.
elements.sent-2411-126: These embeddings are dense, continuous vectors that capture semantic and syntactic information.
elements.sent-2411-127: In models like BERT, GPT, and LLaMA, self-attention mechanisms generate these embeddings, encoding contextual information for each token.
elements.sent-2411-128: Figure \ref{fig:transformer in the Online Appendix illustrates how LLMs generate text embeddings for a sentence from an analyst report on Nvidia.}
elements.sent-2411-129: The embedding dimensions vary: BERT uses 768 or 1,024, GPT-2 Small uses 768, and LLaMA-2-13B model uses 5,120.
elements.sent-2411-130: These high-dimensional representations allow models to capture complex patterns in text, surpassing traditional word-based techniques.
elements.sent-2411-131: To obtain structured representations of analyst reports, I used the pre-trained LLaMA-2-13B model and its tokenizer from MetaAI.
elements.sent-2411-132: The tokenizer processes the text data, converting it into tokens that the model can understand.
elements.sent-2411-133: By processing the analyst reports through this model, I obtained rich, contextual embeddings that capture the nuanced information within the reports.
elements.sent-2411-134: These embeddings are suitable for various downstream NLP tasks, such as sentiment analysis and text classification.
elements.sent-2411-135: The reason I employ LLaMA2 as a baseline Language Model is due to its 4,096-token input limit, which is sufficient for most analyst reports, given their median length of 1,393 tokens and mean length of 2,055 tokens.
elements.sent-2411-136: I use out-of-sample $R^2$ as a measure for the information content of analyst reports.

elements.sent-2411-143: I measure the value of information using equation (\ref{equationiv ) following \citet{Kadan_Manela_Infoval who show that it approximates the value of information in a strategic setting with multiple informed investors, each possessing an imperfect signal based on the theoretical framework of \citet{back2000imperfect : \begin{equation}\widehat{\Omega}_{it} = \frac{r_{it}^2 - \left(r_{it} - \frac{\sum_{j=1}^N \widehat{r}_{ijt}}{N}\right)^2}{\widehat{\lambda}_{it} / p_{it_-}},\label{equationiv}\end{equation} where $\widehat{\Omega _{it}$ is the ratio of explainable realized return variance estimated using Ridge regression to price impact estimated from 1-minute log returns and order flow, multiplied by the closing price of the day prior to the [t-1, t+1] window.\footnote{Due to risk neutrality, the equilibrium of the continuous-time Kyle model holds if $\tilde{v}$ is an unbiased signal of the asset price.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: The intuition is illustrated with an extended single-auction Kyle model incorporating incomplete information in the Online Appendix D.

elements.sent-2411-146: To estimate the price impact $\lambda_{it}$, I perform a regression of 1-minute log returns ($r_{itk}$) on contemporaneous share order flow ($y_{itk}$).

elements.sent-2411-151: The interval $T$ is divided into one-minute sections $\tau_0, \tau_1, \ldots, \tau_K$.
elements.sent-2411-153: I employ the measure for two key reasons.
elements.sent-2411-154: First, the framework is rooted in a general setting of imperfect competition among informed traders, which closely aligns with the reality of financial markets where multiple investors have access to analyst information.
elements.sent-2411-155: This setting is particularly apt for analyzing the value of analyst reports, as it accounts for the correlated nature of information and the strategic interactions among market participants.
elements.sent-2411-156: Second, this measure provides a conservative lower bound for the value estimation, allowing quantification of the minimum potential value of analyst information without overstating its importance.
elements.sent-2411-157: Given that the measure is a ratio of two random variables measured with error, interpretation for information value estimation is inherently nuanced.
elements.sent-2411-158: To tackle this issue, I apply the delta method in estimating the mean and variance of information value over sample $s$.
elements.sent-2411-159: The delta method used to derive the first-order estimator is discussed in the Online Appendix E.
elements.sent-2411-160: To assess the impact of topics within analyst reports, I employ the Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) methodology, as detailed by \citet{lundberg2017unified and applied to financial contexts by \citet{chen2022expected .}}
elements.sent-2411-161: SHAP is a solution concept proposed by \citet{shapley1953value in cooperative game theory that distributes the total payoff generated by a coalition of players among the players themselves.}
elements.sent-2411-162: In my context, this allows me to attribute the overall explanatory power of an analyst report to its constituent topics.
elements.sent-2411-163: Leveraging the additive nature of text embeddings, I design a novel decomposition method based on SHAP values.
elements.sent-2411-164: This approach enables me to systematically assess the relative importance of various topics in analyst reports.
elements.sent-2411-165: First, LLMs pool report-level embeddings by averaging embeddings across all tokens and all layers.
elements.sent-2411-166: The textual embedding can be represented as: \begin{equation}y^{emb} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{e}_{i},\end{equation} where $e_{i}$ is the embedding of token $i$ across all the layers, $N$ is the total number of words in the report.

elements.sent-2411-168: One concern in the architecture of a transformer model is that each token in the input sequence can pay attention to other words through a mechanism called “self-attention” or “scaled dot-product attention.” This mechanism allows the model to weigh the importance of each word relative to every other word in the sequence, thereby capturing contextual information.
elements.sent-2411-169: Consequently, the embedding of a word contains information from other words, which may pertain to different topics.
elements.sent-2411-170: The embedding of the word might predominantly reflect the context information from other topics.
elements.sent-2411-171: To isolate the self-attention across topics, I process the embedding for each sentence in analyst reports and take the token-weighted mean as the report embedding. \begin{equation}y^{emb} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{Token_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}Token_i} y_i^{emb},\end{equation} where \(Token_i\) represents the number of tokens in \(i\)-th sentence and \(y_i^{emb}\) represents the embedding of the \(i\)-th sentence.

elements.subsubsection-2411-011: The Value of Analyst Information around Earnings Announcements
elements.sent-2411-485: Previous evidence underlines the information content of analyst reports in the first week following earnings announcements.
elements.sent-2411-486: To expand on this, I evaluate the information value of analyst reports issued promptly following earnings announcements and beyond that.
elements.sent-2411-487: Figure \ref{fig:iv_ead illustrates the information value of analyst reports between earnings announcements on a weekly basis, from the first week after the earnings announcement up to the $13^{th}$ week.}
elements.sent-2411-488: The information value of analyst reports is highest immediately following earnings announcements, particularly in the first week, peaking at a sample mean of \$0.84 million.
elements.sent-2411-489: This value sharply declines by the second week and stabilizes thereafter, with minor fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-490: These patterns suggest that the market values analyst reports most highly immediately after earnings announcements, with diminishing importance in subsequent weeks.
elements.sent-2411-491: To formally validate the observed pattern, I conduct a regression analysis of log information value on an indicator variable $Week$ which takes the value of one if the report is released within one week following earnings announcements and its interaction with earnings announcement day trading volume ($Trading Volume$).
elements.sent-2411-492: This model is motivated by prior literature suggesting that analysts' interpretative role becomes more prominent with the complexity of financial accounting information (\citeay{chen2010relationship ).}
elements.sent-2411-493: The trading volume serves as a proxy for investor disagreement, following \citet{banerjee2011learning and \citet{beckmann2024unusual .}}
elements.sent-2411-494: The interaction term ($Week \times Trading Volume$) captures how post-earnings timing and investor disagreement jointly affect information value.
elements.sent-2411-495: The model includes stock and year-fixed effects to control for time-invariant stock characteristics and market-wide yearly trends.
elements.sent-2411-496: Table \ref{tab:iv_ead presents regression results showing the influence of earnings announcements and trading volume on information value.}
elements.sent-2411-497: Panel A indicates that the information value is significantly higher for reports released within one week of earnings announcements ($Week$), with positive and significant coefficients across all models.
elements.sent-2411-498: The interaction between $Week$ and $Trading Volume$ also shows a significant positive effect, suggesting that the information value increases with higher trading volumes around earnings announcements.
elements.sent-2411-499: The rationale is that investors may rely more on analyst opinions when there is greater disagreement in the interpretation of earnings information.
elements.sent-2411-500: Panel B reveals significant increases in both log return variance and log price impact following earnings announcements, as evidenced by positive coefficients for the $Week$ variable.
elements.sent-2411-501: The interaction term ($Week \times Trading Volume$) is significantly positive in columns (1)-(4), indicating that explained return volatility rises with higher trading volumes during post-earnings periods.
elements.sent-2411-502: Notably, the increase in the information value of analyst reports following earnings announcements is primarily attributed to higher explained return volatility, rather than changes in price impact.
elements.sent-2411-503: These findings suggest that analyst reports provide more valuable insights in the immediate post-earnings announcement period, particularly when investor uncertainty is high.
elements.sent-2411-504: This increased value likely reflects analysts' ability to interpret complex earnings information, helping investors make sense of the latest financial data during these critical periods.
elements.sent-2411-505: In this paper, I examine both quantitative and qualitative content of analyst reports using $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS }$.}
elements.sent-2411-506: The results show that report text contains more market-valued information content than quantifiable forecast revisions economically, especially right following earnings announcements.
elements.sent-2411-507: A Shapley value decomposition method reveals that analysts' interpretation of realized income statements is the most valuable content within written reports.
elements.sent-2411-508: The text embedding representation extracts more comprehensive information from analyst reports beyond text sentiment.
elements.sent-2411-509: I further translate the information content into dollar terms to provide a sense of economic magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-510: Using a measure of explained return volatility relative to price impact, I find that the annualized expected profit from early information acquisition of the analyst reports for an average S\&P 100 constituent firm is larger than \$6.89 million.
elements.sent-2411-511: While these results are economically meaningful, readers should exercise caution in generalizing beyond the S\&P 100 sample.
elements.sent-2411-512: Cross-sectional analysis reveals that the information value from analysts is higher for large stocks, bold forecasts, and reports released in the week following earnings announcements.
elements.sent-2411-513: These findings have important implications for regulators, particularly concerning the fair distribution of information in financial markets.
elements.sent-2411-514: The significant potential profit from early access to analyst reports raises concerns about selective distribution.
elements.sent-2411-515: Such practices would violate FINRA Rule 2241, which aims to ensure the objectivity of research and prevent the selective distribution of research reports to preferred clients ahead of others.
elements.sent-2411-516: It is crucial to emphasize that this study presents a hypothetical scenario without assessing or endorsing actual market practices.
elements.sent-2411-517: Rather, by quantifying the potential profit of privileged access to analyst information, this research emphasizes the importance of regulations in ensuring fair disclosure.. \onehalfspacing \bibliographystyle{jf.}

The summation across these topics precisely equates to the $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS}}$ of the sentence-segmented report embeddings. The red line represents the SHAP value scaled by the aggregated $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS}}$.}
elements.figure-2411-003: [H] \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{figures/figure4.png} \end{center} \caption{Shapley Values for Sub-Topic Importance}\label{fig:subtopic} { This figure shows the relative importance of sub-topics within income statement analyses discussed in analyst reports, calculated using the Shapley value decomposition approach. The summation of SHAP values for the 18 topics equals the $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS}}$ of the sentence-segmented report embeddings. The red line represents the SHAP value scaled by the summed $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS}}$ for each topic. Panel (a) categorizes income statement analyses into income acquisition and income interpretation, while Panel (b) categorizes them into income realization and income expectation.}
elements.figure-2411-004: [H] \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/iv_ts.png} \end{center} \caption{Dollar Value of Analyst Reports over Time}\label{fig:iv_ts} { This figure presents the estimated value of information, reported in millions of dollars and adjusted to 2020 dollars, derived from analyst reports between 2015Q1 and 2023Q4. Quarterly mean approximations are calculated using the delta method. The solid line represents the combined strategic value of numerical and textual information from analyst reports, while the dashed line shows the strategic value of textual information alone. The horizontal line marks the sample mean over the 2015-2023 period. The light blue shaded area indicates the 95\% confidence interval.}
elements.figure-2411-005: [H] \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/iv_ead.png} \end{center} \caption{Information Value of Analyst Reports after Earnings Announcements}\label{fig:iv_ead} { This figure presents the estimated information value of analyst reports released 1-13 weeks following the most recent earnings announcement. The numbers are reported in millions of dollars and adjusted to 2020 dollars. The sample spans from 2015Q1 to 2023Q4. The vertical axis indicates the value of information, while the horizontal axis represents weeks post-earnings announcements. Each point reflects the average value of both numerical and textual information in analyst reports for the respective week, calculated using the delta method. The solid green line marks the sample mean value of information, with green bars showing the 95\% confidence intervals. The red dashed horizontal line represents the sample mean over the 2015-2023 period.}

elements.figure-2411-008: \caption{OLS Regressions of Numerical and Textual Information}\label{tab:ols} { This table presents OLS regression results analyzing the information content of analyst reports. Panel A shows the summary statistics of the variables. Panel B reports coefficient estimates and t-statistics. The dependent variable, $CAR_{[-1,+1]}$, represents the three-day cumulative abnormal returns centered on the report release date, with abnormal returns calculated as the raw return minus the buy-and-hold return on the NYSE/AMEX/NASDAQ value-weighted market return. REC\_REV denotes recommendation revision, calculated as the current report's recommendation minus the last recommendation in I/B/E/S issued by the same analyst for the same stock. EF\_REV represents earnings forecast revision, calculated as the current report's EPS forecast minus the last EPS forecast in I/B/E/S issued by the same analyst for the same stock, scaled by the stock price 50 days before the report release. TP\_REV indicates target price revision, calculated as the current report's target price minus the last target price in I/B/E/S issued by the same analyst for the same stock, scaled by the stock price 50 days before the report release. $Prior\_CAR$ represents the cumulative abnormal return over time period [-10, -2]. $SUE$ denotes the earnings surprise. $\widehat{CAR}_{txt}$ is the out-of-sample fitted value of Ridge regression using full-context report embeddings. $\widehat{CAR}_{rev}$ is the out-of-sample fitted value of Ridge regression using the three forecast revision measures. All explanatory variables are standardized in the regressions. The t-statistics, presented in parentheses, are calculated using a two-way clustering method by stock and year. $* p<.1$, $** p<.05$, $*** p<.01$.} \begin{center} \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{l*{6}{Y}} \multicolumn{7}{l}{Panel A: Summary statistics} {} Mean Std p25 P50 P75 N $CAR_{[-1,+1]}$ 0.002 0.048 -0.019 0.001 0.021 28837 REC\_REV 0.002 0.151 0.000 0.000 0.000 28837 EF\_REV 0.000 0.005 0.000 0.000 0.001 28837 TP\_REV 0.010 0.067 0.000 0.000 0.000 28837 $\widehat{CAR\_{rev}}$ 0.002 0.014 -0.001 -0.000 0.003 28837 $\widehat{CAR\_{txt}}$ 0.002 0.018 -0.009 0.002 0.013 28837 \end{tabularx} \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{l*{7}{Y}} \multicolumn{8}{l}{Panel B: Market reaction to forecast revisions and report text} \multicolumn{1}{c}{(1)} \multicolumn{1}{c}{(2)} \multicolumn{1}{c}{(3)} \multicolumn{1}{c}{(4)} \multicolumn{1}{c}{(5)} \multicolumn{1}{c}{(6)} \multicolumn{1}{c}{(7)} REC\_REV 0.001\sym{***} 0.001\sym{**} (3.26) (2.06) EF\_REV 0.005\sym{***} 0.004\sym{***} (7.72) (5.39) TP\_REV 0.012\sym{***} 0.009\sym{***} (14.95) (11.71) $\widehat{CAR\_{txt}}$ 0.015\sym{***} 0.012\sym{***} 0.012\sym{***} 0.012\sym{***} 0.012\sym{***} (24.23) (22.40) (22.30) (19.13) (22.62) $\widehat{CAR\_{rev}}$ 0.014\sym{***} 0.010\sym{***} 0.010\sym{***} 0.010\sym{***} (18.29) (14.00) (13.44) (13.35) SUE 0.001\sym{***} 0.001\sym{**} (3.97) (2.36) Prior\_CAR -0.003\sym{***} -0.003\sym{***} (-4.45) (-5.01) Constant 0.002\sym{***} 0.002\sym{***} 0.002\sym{***} 0.002\sym{***} 0.002\sym{***} 0.002\sym{***} 0.002\sym{***} (3.14) (2.82) (3.09) (3.11) (3.07) (3.41) (3.83) Year FE No No No No No No Yes Stock FE No No No No No No Yes \(N\) 28837 28837 28837 28837 28837 23314 20987 Adjusted \(R^{2}\) 0.091 0.105 0.149 0.089 0.147 0.137 0.146 \end{tabularx} \end{center}

elements.figure-2411-013: \caption{Summary Statistics for Analyst Information Value}\label{tab:iv_ss} { This table presents the summary statistics for the information values of analyst reports for constituent stocks of the S\&P 100 index over the period from 2015Q1 to 2023Q4. The value of information is measured as the explained return volatility divided by the price impact, with the results reported in millions of dollars. All dollar values are adjusted for inflation to reflect 2020 values using the Consumer Price Index (CPI). Panel A reports the dollar value of analysts' information, the information value derived from textual content, and the information value attributable to forecast revisions. Both mean and standard deviation are computed using the delta method. Panel B reports the price impact per billion dollars and the stock price. The price impact is estimated through a regression of one-minute log returns on contemporaneous share order flow, normalized by the closing stock price two trading days prior to report release (t-2), and is reported in billions of dollars. The stock price refers to the closing stock price two trading days prior to the report release (t-2).} \begin{center} \tabcolsep = 0.23cm \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2} \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{lYYYYY} \multicolumn{6}{l}{Panel A: Dollar value of analysts information} {} Mean SE 95\%CI 99\%CI N Information value, \$M 0.47 0.05 [0.38, 0.56] [0.35, 0.58] 17672 Information value of text, \$M 0.38 0.04 [0.30, 0.46] [0.28, 0.48] 17672 Information value of revisions, \$M 0.34 0.04 [0.26, 0.43] [0.23, 0.46] 17672 \end{tabularx} \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{lYYYYYYY} \multicolumn{8}{l}{Panel B: Price impact and stock price} {} {} Mean Std p25 P50 P75 N Price impact per \$B {} 0.34 1.29 0.05 0.13 0.31 17672 Stock price {} 118.49 130.34 51.15 82.0 143.58 17672 \end{tabularx} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-014: \caption{Value of Analyst Information and Stock Characteristics} { This table shows the results of regressing the log information value and its components on stock characteristics. Log information value (text) specifically measures the value of information estimated using analyst report text. The log information value equals log explained return volatility less log price impact, which are used as dependent variables in Panel B. Observations with negative price impact and negative explained return variance are omitted. Variable definitions are presented in Table \ref{tab:numdef}. elements.figure-2411-021: [H] \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/transformer_blue.jpg} \end{center} \caption{Large Language Model Architecture}\label{fig:transformer} { This figure shows an example of how large language models process a sentence from an analyst report on Nvidia. The model breaks this sentence into tokens, and then generates two types of embeddings: token embeddings and position embeddings. These embeddings then pass through the core of the model - a stack of transformer layers. The output of each transformer layer is a series of vectors representing the tokens in their full context.}
elements.figure-2411-022: [H] \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/iv_ind.png} \end{center} \caption{Analyst Information Value by Industry}\label{fig:iv_ind} { This figure shows the estimated information value of analyst reports for Fama-French 12 industries from 2015Q1 to 2023Q4, quantified in millions of dollars. The solid green line marks the sample mean value of information, with green bars showing the 95\% confidence intervals. The horizontal dashed red line marks the sample mean of stocks from all industries. Information value estimates are derived using the delta method and adjusted to 2020 levels using the Consumer Price Index (CPI).}
elements.figure-2411-023: [H] \begin{center} \includegraphics[width = \textwidth]{figures/figurea2.png} \end{center} \caption{Shapley Values Scaled by Length}\label{fig: shap_scale} { This figure presents the SHAP values of topics scaled by the total number of sentences (Panel A) and the total number of tokens (Panel B) for topics with more than 100,000 sentences. The scaling process involves two steps: First, normalize the Shapley value of each topic by dividing it by the number of sentences in that topic. Then, adjust the normalized values to ensure the total sum of SHAP values remains consistent. This ensures that the scaled values reflect the relative importance of topics while preserving the magnitude of total $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS}}$.}
elements.figure-2411-024: [H] \begin{center} \includegraphics[width = 0.9\textwidth]{figures/figure3.png} \end{center} \caption{$R^2_{\mathrm{OOS}}$ of Sentence-Segmented Embeddings by Year}\label{fig:shap_yearly} { This figure shows the $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS}}$ of sentence-segmented report embeddings from 2015 to 2023. The color gradient indicates the magnitude of $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS}}$. The red line represents the SHAP value of the Income Statements Analyses topic across the years. The shaded area marks the pandemic recession in 2020.}
elements.figure-2411-025: [H] \begin{center} \includegraphics[width = \textwidth]{figures/figurea1.png} \end{center} \caption{Shapley Values for Topic Importance across Industries}\label{fig: shap_ind} { This figure shows the relative importance of 17 topics in analyst reports across the Fama-French 12 industries, quantified using the SHAP value. For each industry, the bars represent the SHAP values of different topics, with their sum equaling the $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS}}$ of the sentence-segmented report embeddings. The red line shows each topic's SHAP value as a proportion of the total $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS}}$.}
elements.figure-2411-026: [H] \begin{center} \includegraphics[width = \textwidth]{figures/figurea3.png} \end{center} \caption{Shapley Values for Topic Importance by Analyst Characteristics}\label{fig: shap_ana} { This figure illustrates how 17 different topics contribute to the predictive power of analyst reports across four subsamples, measured using SHAP values. The subsamples compare analysts with above-median (Panel A) versus below-median work experience (Panel B), and large (Panel C) versus small brokerage firms (Panel D), with subsamples determined by yearly median. For each subsample, the bars show the SHAP values of individual topics, whose sum equals the out-of-sample R-squared ($R^2_{\mathrm{OOS}}$) from the sentence-segmented report embeddings. The red line indicates each topic's relative importance, calculated as the topic's SHAP value divided by the total $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS}}$.}
elements.figure-2411-027: [H] \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/iv_ts_robust.png} \end{center} \caption{Alternative Analyst Information Value Estimations Over Time}\label{fig:iv_ts_robust} { This figure presents the alternative estimations of analyst information value from 2015Q1 to 2024Q4, reported in millions of dollars and adjusted to 2020 dollars. Quarterly mean approximations are calculated using the delta method. TAQ volatility indicates the explained return volatility is calculated as $\frac{r^2 - (r - \widehat{r})^2}{r^2} \cdot \sigma_v^2$, where $\sigma_v^2$ is the realized volatility from one-minute log returns. EMO and CLNV refer to trade-signing algorithms developed by \citet{ellis2000underwriter} and \citet{lee1991inferring}, respectively.}
elements.figure-2411-028: [H] \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/iv_stock.png} \end{center} \caption{Analyst Information Value by Stock}\label{fig:iv_stock} { This figure shows the estimated information value of analyst reports for individual stocks from 2015Q1 to 2023Q4, quantified in millions of dollars. Estimates are derived using the delta method. Market equity, averaged daily at the time of each report release, is reported in billions of dollars. All values are adjusted to 2020 levels using the Consumer Price Index (CPI).}
elements.figure-2411-029: \caption{Numerical Variables Description}\label{tab:numdef} { This table shows the definitions of numerical measures.} \begin{center} \tabcolsep = 0.45cm \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2} \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}p{4.5cm}p{11cm}@{}} Numerical Measures Definition and/or sources Panel A: Firm Level Measures Size The market value equity of the firm ($CSHOQ * PRCCQ$) at the end of the quarter prior to report release. BtoM The book value of equity ($SEQ + TXDB + ITCB -PREF$) scaled by the market value of equity ($CSHOQ * PRCCQ$) t the end of quarter prior the report release. Prior\_CAR The cumulated 10-day abnormal return ending 2 days before release. The abnormal return is calculated as the raw return minus the buy-and-hold market value-weighted return. SUE Earnings surprise, calculated as the actual EPS minus the last consensus EPS forecast before the earnings announcement. Consensus EPS is the median value of 1-year EPS forecast within 90-days window of all analysts following the firm. The unexpected earnings is scaled by price per share at the fiscal quarter end. AbsSUE Absolue value of SUE, representing the distance between realized EPS and EPS expectation. Miss Dummy variable that equals one if the actual EPS is less than the last consensus forecast, and 0 otherwise. Trading volume Trading volume at earnings announcement day (or the first trading day post earnings announcement), calculated as $VOL/SHROUT$. Distance to default The distance to default calculated following \citet{merton1974pricing}. The proxy is compiled from the National University of Singapore's Credit Research Initiative (NUS CRI). Fluidity A measure of firms' product market competition introduced by \citet{hoberg2014product}. The data is compiled from the Hoberg and Phillips database. Panel B: Industry Level Measures Industry recession An indicator variable that equals one if the FF-48 industry return is negative and in the bottom quintile of FF-48 industry returns and zero otherwise. Panel C: Macroeconomic Measures Time trend (ttr) The number of years elapsed from the beginning of the sample. Panel D: Report Level Measures $REC\_REV$ Recommendation revision, calculated as the current report's recommendation minus the last recommendation in I/B/E/S issued by the same analyst for the same firm. $EF\_REV$ Earnings forecast revision, calculated as the current report's EPS forecast minus the last EPS forecast in I/B/E/S issued by the same analyst for the same firm, scaled by the stock price 50 days before the report release. $TP\_REV$ Target price revision, calculated as the current report's target price minus the last target price in I/B/E/S issued by the same analyst for the same firm, scaled by the stock price 50 days before the report release. Boldness An indicator variable that takes the value of 1 if the EPS forecast revision is above both the analyst's own prior forecast and the consensus forecast, or else below both, and zero otherwise. SR Stock recommendation from I/B/E/S rating, with 1 being the most bullish (Strong Buy) and 5 being the most bearish (Sell), based on the ratings provided by the Institutional Brokers' Estimate System (I/B/E/S). ERet 12-month return forecast by scaling the 12-month price target by the stock price 1-day before release. \end{tabularx} \end{center}

elements.sent-2411-518: Ridge regression addresses multicollinearity by adding a regularization term to the least squares objective function.
elements.sent-2411-519: The ridge regression estimator is given by: \begin{equation}\widehat{\beta} = \underset{\beta}{\operatorname{argmin}} \left\{ \|y_i - x_i^T \beta\|_2^2 + \alpha \|\beta\|_2^2 \right\},\end{equation} where \( \alpha \) is the regularization parameter that controls the trade-off between fitting the data and shrinking the coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-520: To find the optimal value of \( \alpha \), cross-validation is used over a grid of values ranging from \( 10^{-10 \) to \( 10^{10 \).}}
elements.sent-2411-521: The cross-validation process ensures that the chosen model generalizes well to unseen data, preventing overfitting while capturing the predictive power of the text embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-522: To mitigate the risk of overfitting inherent in high-dimensional text embeddings, I employ Partial Least Squares (PLS) for dimensionality reduction.
elements.sent-2411-523: The optimization problem can be expressed as follows: \begin{equation}\hat{\beta} = \underset{\beta}{\operatorname{argmin}} \left( \| (\Omega^T X_i) \beta - y_i \|_2^2 \right),\end{equation} where $\Omega$ is a $K \times P$ transformation matrix that reduces the $K$ predictors in \( X_i \) to $P$ lower-dimensional components.
elements.sent-2411-524: The extraction of the \( j \)-th PLS component is guided by the following objective function: \begin{equation}\omega_j = \underset{\omega}{\text{argmax}} \, \text{Cov}(Y, X\omega), \quad \text{s.t.} \,\, \omega'\omega = 1, \,\, \text{Cov}(X\omega, X\omega_i) = 0 \,\, \forall i < j.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\omega_j = \underset{\omega}{argmax} \, Cov(Y, X\omega), \quad s.t. \,\, \omega'\omega = 1, \,\, Cov(X\omega, X\omega_i) = 0 \,\, \forall i < j.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-525: In essence, this approach sequentially extracts components that maximize the covariance with the outcome variable, while ensuring orthogonality to previously extracted components.
elements.sent-2411-526: Tree-based approaches are commonly applied in stock return forecasting literature (see, e.g., \citeay{gu2020empirical ; \citeay{cao2024man ; \citeay{bonini2023value ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-527: XGBoost is an advanced implementation of tree-based machine learning models that builds an ensemble of decision trees.
elements.sent-2411-528: In XGBoost, each tree is added sequentially to correct the errors of previous trees.
elements.sent-2411-529: The main idea is to combine the outputs of multiple weak learners (decision trees) to create a strong learner.
elements.sent-2411-530: XGBoost incorporates regularization techniques, such as L1 (Lasso) and L2 (Ridge), to prevent overfitting and enhance model generalization.
elements.sent-2411-531: In comparison to random forests, which build a multitude of independent trees and aggregate their predictions, XGBoost constructs trees sequentially, with each tree designed to correct the errors of the preceding ones.
elements.sent-2411-532: While random forests rely on bagging, a method that combines the predictions of various trees to reduce variance, XGBoost uses boosting, an approach that aims to reduce both bias and variance by focusing on difficult-to-predict instances in subsequent iterations.
elements.sent-2411-533: This boosting approach allows XGBoost to effectively capture and leverage the nuanced information embedded in textual data, leading to a more accurate estimation of stock returns.
elements.sent-2411-534: To extend beyond the linear modeling approach, I explore the use of Neural Networks to estimate $CAR$ using text embeddings derived from analyst reports.
elements.sent-2411-535: A Neural Network can capture complex non-linear relationships between the text embeddings and the $CAR$, potentially improving the model's fitting capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-536: Consider a three-layer Neural Network as the prediction model.
elements.sent-2411-537: The prediction problem can be formulated as follows: \begin{equation}f(X_i; \theta) = W_3 \sigma\left(W_2 \sigma\left(W_1 X_i + b_1\right) + b_2\right) + b_3, \label{eqnn1}\end{equation} where $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the ReLU activation function, $W_i$ and $b_i$ represent the weights and biases for layer $i$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-538: The Neural Network architecture consists of an input layer representing the text embeddings, followed by multiple hidden layers.
elements.sent-2411-539: The specific architecture employed includes 32 neurons in the first hidden layer, followed by 16, 8, 4, and 2 neurons in subsequent layers.
elements.sent-2411-540: This structure is flexible and can be adjusted by adding or removing layers as necessary to optimize performance.
elements.sent-2411-541: The training process involves optimizing the weights and biases to minimize the loss function, which, in this case, is the mean-square loss.
elements.sent-2411-542: To regularize the model and prevent overfitting, early stopping is implemented, halting training once the validation loss ceases to decrease.
elements.sent-2411-543: Additionally, following \citet{gu2020empirical , the models are retrained five times, and the final estimation is obtained by averaging the outputs of these five models, forming an ensemble estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-544: I provide an intuition for the measure of strategic value by discussing a simple extension of \citet{kyle1985continuous model.}
elements.sent-2411-545: In the Kyle model, there is one risky asset with a payoff $\tilde{v \sim N\left(p_0, \Sigma_0\right)$.}
elements.sent-2411-546: Three types of traders exist: a strategic trader with insider information, a market maker who sets prices in a perfectly competitive market, and an uninformed trader who trades $\tilde{u \sim N\left(0, \sigma_u^2\right)$, where $\tilde{u}$ is independent of $\tilde{v}$.}
elements.sent-2411-547: Illiquidity is measured by Kyle's lambda ($\lambda$).
elements.sent-2411-548: Kyle's lambda depends on private information and liquidity trading.
elements.sent-2411-549: I extend the model by considering a case where $\phi$ percentage of variance in $\tilde{v}$ is explained by the informed trader's information.
elements.sent-2411-551: The asset is traded with asymmetric information at period $t_0$, and the value $\tilde{v}$ is realized at period $t_1$.
elements.sent-2411-552: I assume without loss of generality that \begin{equation}\tilde{v} = P_0 + \tilde{s} + \tilde{\epsilon},\end{equation} where $\tilde{s}$ is a mean-zero signal observed by the informed trader at $t_0$, and $\tilde{\epsilon}$ is the combination of residual information and noise.
elements.sent-2411-553: I assume that the signal $\tilde{s}$ and residual $\tilde{\epsilon}$ are uncorrelated, that is, $\sigma_v^2 = \sigma_s^2 + \sigma_\epsilon^2$.. Let \begin{equation}\phi=\frac{\operatorname{var}(\tilde{s})}{\operatorname{var}\left(\tilde{v}\right)} = \frac{\sigma_s^2}{\sigma_v^2} .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-554: This measure $\phi$ is the "R-square" of projecting $\tilde{v}$ on $\tilde{s}$, or the percentage of explainable variance in $\tilde{v}$ using signal $\tilde{s}$.
elements.sent-2411-555: After observing $\tilde{s}$, the informed trader submits a market order $\tilde{x =X(\tilde{s )$, and the uninformed trader trades a zero-mean random variable $\tilde{z}$ that is normally distributed and independent of $\tilde{v}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-556: The profit of the informed trader is given by: $\tilde{\pi =(\tilde{v -\tilde{p )\tilde{v}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-557: The insider has a rational guess of $P(\tilde{x +\tilde{u )$ and understands that his order $\tilde{x}$ will move the price against him.}}
elements.sent-2411-558: The market maker observes the order flow $\tilde{y \stackrel{\text{def {= \tilde{x + \tilde{z}$ and determines the equilibrium price $\tilde{p =P(\tilde{x +\tilde{u )$ to break even.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-559: The assumptions for the market maker are risk neutrality and perfect competition, which drives the profits for market makers to zero.
elements.sent-2411-560: An equilibrium is a set of $X$ and $P$ satisfying \begin{equation}E[\tilde{\pi}(X, P) \mid \tilde{s}=s],\end{equation} \begin{equation}\tilde{p}(X, P)=E[\tilde{v} \mid \tilde{x}+\tilde{u}].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-561: The first condition is profit maximization, stating that given the market maker's pricing rule, the insider chooses a strategy $X$ maximizing her conditional expected profit, taking into account the pricing rule.
elements.sent-2411-562: The second condition is market efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-563: Given the insiders' trading strategy, the market maker sets the price to be the expected value of the security.
elements.sent-2411-564: Conjecture: \begin{equation} P(\tilde{y})=\mu+\lambda \tilde{y}, X(\tilde{s})=\alpha+\beta \tilde{s}. \end{equation} The profit of the insider can be written as: \begin{equation} E[\tilde{\pi}(X, P) \mid \tilde{s}=s] = E[(\tilde{v}-\mu-\lambda(\tilde{u}+x)) x \mid \tilde{s}=s] =(P_0 + s + -\mu-\lambda x) x. \end{equation} Traders take into account that her order flow will move the price against her, which serves to restrain her position size.
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation} E[\tilde{\pi}(X, P) \mid \tilde{s}=s] = E[(\tilde{v}-\mu-\lambda(\tilde{u}+x)) x \mid \tilde{s}=s] =(P_0 + s + -\mu-\lambda x) x. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-565: Solving for optimal profit, I get: \begin{equation}x^*=\frac{P_0 + s-\mu}{2 \lambda}=\alpha+\beta v.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-566: Hence, I can express $\alpha$ and $\beta$ : \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \beta=\frac{1}{2 \lambda}, \alpha=\frac{P_0-\mu}{2 \lambda}= (P_0-\mu) \beta. \end{gathered}\end{equation} When the market maker puts a higher weight on the order flow in setting the price, the trader puts a lower weight on her information.
elements.sent-2411-567: I now look at the price-setting rule: \begin{equation}\mu+\lambda y=E\{\tilde{v} \mid \alpha+\beta \tilde{s}+\tilde{u}=y\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-568: Essentially, the market maker observes a normally distributed signal about \begin{equation} \mu + \lambda y = E[\tilde{v} \mid y] =\bar{v}+\frac{\operatorname{cov}(\tilde{v}, \alpha+\beta \tilde{s}+\tilde{u})}{\operatorname{var}(\alpha+\beta \tilde{s}+\tilde{u})}(\beta \tilde{s}-\beta \bar{s}+\tilde{u}) = p_0 + \frac{\beta \sigma_s^2}{\beta^2 \sigma_s^2 + \sigma_u^2} (y - \alpha). \end{equation} Hence, I can express $\lambda$ and $\mu$ : \begin{equation} \lambda=\frac{\beta \sigma_s^2}{\beta^2 \sigma_s^2 + \sigma_u^2}, \mu= p_0-\alpha \lambda. \end{equation} There is a unique linear equilibrium given by \begin{equation} \mu= P_0, \lambda=\frac{\sigma_s}{2 \sigma_u}, \alpha= 0, \beta=\frac{\sigma_u}{\sigma_s}. \end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-569: The parameter $\lambda$ is universally known as Kyle's lambda.
elements.sent-2411-570: Formally, it is the impact on the equilibrium price of a unit order.
elements.sent-2411-571: Its reciprocal ($1 / \lambda$) measures the liquidity (or depth) of the market.
elements.sent-2411-572: If $1 / \lambda = \frac{2 \sigma_u {\sigma_s}$ is larger, then the market is more liquid, either because there is less private information in $\sigma_s$ or there is more liquid trade in the sense of $\sigma_u$.}
elements.sent-2411-573: Notice that the equilibrium strategy of the informed trader is $\tilde{x =\beta s$.}
elements.sent-2411-574: The unconditional expected gain of the informed trader is \begin{equation} E[\tilde{x}[\tilde{v}-p(\tilde{x}+\tilde{z})]] =\beta E[s(\tilde{v}-\mu-\lambda \beta s -\lambda \tilde{u})] =\beta(1-\lambda \beta) \sigma_s^2 =\frac{\sigma_s \sigma_u}{2} =\frac{\phi \sigma_v^2}{4\lambda}. \end{equation} The expected gain for the informed trader is maximized when she has more private information or when there is more liquidity trading.
elements.sent-2411-575: On the other hand, liquidity traders incur losses equivalent to the informed trader's gains, but they accept these losses due to other motives for trading.
elements.sent-2411-576: The equilibrium price ensures that market makers do not profit or lose in expectation.
elements.sent-2411-577: I use the delta method to approximate the expected value and variance of a ratio of two random variables.
elements.sent-2411-578: This method relies on a first-order Taylor expansion.
elements.sent-2411-579: Specifically, consider two random variables $X$ and $Y$ with means $\mu_X$ and $\mu_Y$, and variances $\sigma_X^2$ and $\sigma_Y^2$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-580: We are interested in the ratio $Z = \frac{X {Y}$ and want to approximate the mean $E[Z]$ and the variance $\text{Var (Z)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-581: The key idea is to approximate the function $g(X, Y) = \frac{X {Y}$ using a Taylor series expansion around the means $\mu_X$ and $\mu_Y$.}
elements.sent-2411-582: For the function $g(X, Y)$, I use a first-order Taylor expansion around $(\mu_X, \mu_Y)$: \begin{equation}g(X, Y) \approx g(\mu_X, \mu_Y) + \left. \frac{\partial g}{\partial X} \right|_{(\mu_X, \mu_Y)} (X - \mu_X) + \left. \frac{\partial g}{\partial Y} \right|_{(\mu_X, \mu_Y)} (Y - \mu_Y).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-030: \begin{equation}g(X, Y) \approx g(\mu_X, \mu_Y) + \left. \frac{\partial g}{\partial X} \right|_{(\mu_X, \mu_Y)} (X - \mu_X) + \left. \frac{\partial g}{\partial Y} \right|_{(\mu_X, \mu_Y)} (Y - \mu_Y).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-583: The partial derivatives of $g(X, Y) = \frac{X {Y}$ are: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial g}{\partial X} = \frac{1}{Y}, \quad \frac{\partial g}{\partial Y} = -\frac{X}{Y^2}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-584: Evaluating these at $(\mu_X, \mu_Y)$, I get: \begin{equation}\left. \frac{\partial g}{\partial X} \right|_{(\mu_X, \mu_Y)} = \frac{1}{\mu_Y}, \quad \left. \frac{\partial g}{\partial Y} \right|_{(\mu_X, \mu_Y)} = -\frac{\mu_X}{\mu_Y^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-585: Substituting the partial derivatives into the Taylor expansion, I obtain: \begin{equation}\frac{X}{Y} \approx \frac{\mu_X}{\mu_Y} + \frac{1}{\mu_Y} (X - \mu_X) - \frac{\mu_X}{\mu_Y^2} (Y - \mu_Y).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-586: Taking the expectation on both sides: \begin{equation}E\left[ \frac{X}{Y} \right] \approx \frac{\mu_X}{\mu_Y} + \frac{1}{\mu_Y} E[X - \mu_X] - \frac{\mu_X}{\mu_Y^2} E[Y - \mu_Y].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-587: Since $E[X - \mu_X] = 0$ and $E[Y - \mu_Y] = 0$, the approximation simplifies to: \begin{equation}E\left[ \frac{X}{Y} \right] \approx \frac{\mu_X}{\mu_Y}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-588: Using the delta method, the variance of $Z=\frac{X {Y}$ is approximated by: \begin{equation}\operatorname{Var}(Z) \approx\left(\frac{\partial g}{\partial X}\right)^2 \operatorname{Var}(X)+\left(\frac{\partial g}{\partial Y}\right)^2 \operatorname{Var}(Y)+2\left(\frac{\partial g}{\partial X}\right)\left(\frac{\partial g}{\partial Y}\right) \operatorname{Cov}(X, Y) .\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-036: \begin{equation}\operatorname{Var}(Z) \approx\left(\frac{\partial g}{\partial X}\right)^2 \operatorname{Var}(X)+\left(\frac{\partial g}{\partial Y}\right)^2 \operatorname{Var}(Y)+2\left(\frac{\partial g}{\partial X}\right)\left(\frac{\partial g}{\partial Y}\right) \operatorname{Cov}(X, Y) .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-590: In the context of the provided study, I estimate the mean value of information for a subsample $s$ using the delta method.
elements.sent-2411-591: Let $\widehat{\Omega _{it = \frac{\widehat{\sigma _{it ^2 {\widehat{\lambda _{i t / P_{i t_- = \frac{r_{i f ^2-\left(r_{i t -\frac{\sum_{j=1 ^N \widehat{r _{i j t {N}\right)^2 {\widehat{\lambda _{i t / P_{i t_- }$ be the value of information for stock $i$ on date $t$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-592: Define the mean and variance over subsample $s$ as: \begin{equation}\mu_{\nu s} = \frac{1}{|s|} \sum_{it \in s} \left(r_{i f}^2-\left(r_{i t}-\frac{\sum_{j=1}^N \widehat{r}_{i j t}}{N}\right)^2\right),\end{equation} and the mean price impact per dollar over subsample $s$ as: \begin{equation}\mu_{\lambda s} = \frac{1}{|s|} \sum_{it \in s} \frac{\widehat{\lambda}_{it}}{P_{i t_-}}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-038: \begin{equation}\mu_{\nu s} = \frac{1}{|s|} \sum_{it \in s} \left(r_{i f}^2-\left(r_{i t}-\frac{\sum_{j=1}^N \widehat{r}_{i j t}}{N}\right)^2\right),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-593: Using the delta method approximation for the mean of the ratio, the mean value of information over subsample $s$ is given by: \begin{equation}E\widehat{\Omega}_s \approx \frac{\mu_{\nu s}}{\mu_{\lambda s}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-595: This provides a first-order approximation for the mean and variance of the value of information over the subsample $s$ using the delta method.
elements.sent-2411-596: Prior studies have shed light on the incremental information value of report text through sentiment analyses (\citeay{asquith2005information ; \citeay{huang2014evidence ; \citeay{huang2018analyst ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-597: However, analyst reports are meant to provide contextual information beyond simple sentiment dimension and the context has been perceived at least as important as the quantifiable outputs.
elements.sent-2411-598: According to the annual survey of Institutional Investor magazine, investors consistently rank ``Written Reports'' as more valuable than earnings forecasts and stock recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-599: Yet, empirical evidence supporting this survey feedback is limited, and it remains unclear what specific discussion holds the most value for investors.
elements.sent-2411-600: The advent of large language models (LLMs) has significantly enhanced our ability to quantify textual meaning beyond sentiment and topics, allowing us to capture both contextual information and the reasoning logic within the text (\citeay{chen2022expected ; \citeay{li2024dissecting ).}}
elements.sent-2411-601: For example, consider the following two statements: ``Reported EPS of \$0.94 miss consensus of \$1.40 and our estimate of \$1.17.'' and ``Reported 1Q12 MS franchise sales are below our estimate, as sales were negatively impacted by unfavorable distribution channel dynamics.'' While both sentences express negative sentiment and pertain to the ``earnings analysis" topic, the latter provides additional contextual information and reasoning that can be interpreted and analyzed by LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-602: I quantify the value of analyst information in analyst reports through three general steps: analyst output representation, econometric modeling, and value decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-603: Each report is mapped into a structured embedding space using the LLMs' transformer architecture.
elements.sent-2411-604: With structured text representation, I evaluate how well analyst information explains stock returns using an R-squared measure, which also serves as the terminology of information content.
elements.sent-2411-605: Additionally, I evaluate the out-of-sample explanatory power of quantitative and qualitative measures for abnormal stock returns to assess model stability.
elements.sent-2411-606: I find that the textual information generates an out-of-sample $R^2$ of 10.19\% for three-day cumulative abnormal returns ($CAR$), surpassing the 9.01\% explained by numerical information.
elements.sent-2411-607: These findings remain robust across various LLM text representations, machine learning algorithms, and $CAR$ windows, highlighting the substantial and unique information content within analyst report text.
elements.sent-2411-608: Both qualitative and quantitative information provide greater value to the financial market when linked to forecast revisions.
elements.sent-2411-609: A notable amount of research emphasizes the role of analysts in information processing around corporate earnings announcements, e.g., \citet{livnat2012information , \citet{keskek2014analyst , \citet{kim2015management , \citet{lobo2017effect , and \citet{barron2017earnings , An investigation on samples within and beyond the earnings announcement windows reveals that the information content of analyst reports peaks in the first week following earnings announcements.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-610: The finding supports \citet{huang2018analyst 's perspective that timely reactions to corporate disclosures offer valuable information advantages to clients.}
elements.sent-2411-611: Using separately trained models on samples within and beyond earnings announcement windows, the magnitude of the out-of-sample $R^2$ nearly doubles in the first week following earnings announcement dates compared to other periods.
elements.sent-2411-612: The significant increase underscores the incremental value that analysts add by interpreting and contextualizing earnings information for investors.
elements.sent-2411-613: Analysts might simply be ``piggybacking'' on information disclosed during earnings conference calls, I incorporate controls for both the earnings surprise and the text embeddings of the latest earnings conference call transcripts into models to isolate the unique information from analyst reports.
elements.sent-2411-614: The findings indicate that even after accounting for the information from conference calls, analyst reports contain distinct and valuable insights.
elements.sent-2411-615: To systematically quantify the importance of different content in analyst reports, I leverage the additive feature of text embeddings and design a Shapley value decomposition approach to fully attribute the explanatory power to 17 major topics discussed in analyst reports.
elements.sent-2411-616: So far, I have measured information value statistically with R-squared.
elements.sent-2411-617: To translate it into economic terms, I adopt \citet{Kadan_Manela_Infoval 's framework to quantify the dollar value of analyst information to strategic investors.}
elements.sent-2411-618: The information value can be interpreted as the total expected profits for strategic investors either because: (1) some investors have early access to analyst views, or (2) they conduct independent research and derive insights comparable to those of analysts.
elements.sent-2411-619: This multi-client framework provides a realistic foundation for analyzing the complex dynamics of analyst reports dissemination.
elements.sent-2411-620: My paper relates to three strands of literature.
elements.sent-2411-621: This paper first relates to recent studies on the nature of information conveyed by analysts through their qualitative outputs (\citeay{huang2018analyst ; \citeay{chen2023revisiting ; \citeay{chi2024use ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-622: My work provides evidence for the superiority of state-of-the-art LLMs in generating contextualized textual representations.
elements.sent-2411-623: Furthermore, transitioning from tone analysis alone to a full-text representation using LLM embeddings further boosts the $R^2$ measure from 3\% to above 10\%.
elements.sent-2411-624: The significant improvement in explanatory power demonstrates the potential of LLMs to revolutionize the analysis of textual information.
elements.sent-2411-625: Topic decomposition analysis offers a new dimension for understanding analyst information value by revealing their role in interpreting financial data.
elements.sent-2411-626: Recent studies like \citet{chen2022expected , \citet{li2024dissecting , \citet{jha2024chatgpt , and \citet{beckmann2024unusual have showcased the potential of implementing LLMs in financial contexts.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-627: This paper extends prior work by quantifying the economic dollar value of such insider information.
elements.sent-2411-628: In this section, I introduce how I employ large language models for topic modeling and text embeddings, the information content estimation, the strategic information value estimation, and the decomposition metric used to evaluate the information value of topics.\footnote{The details of machine learning models used to explain contemporaneous stock returns are discussed in the Online Appendix C.}
elements.sent-2411-629: I harness the power of advanced generative AI models to conduct comprehensive analyses of analyst reports.

elements.subsubsection-2411-015: What Content in Analyst Reports is Valued?
elements.sent-2411-1006: Having established the significance of report text information, I examine which specific content components most influence market response.

elements.sent-2411-1009: When switching from full-context to sentence-segmented embeddings, the $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS }$ decreases by 2.25\%, quantifying the magnitude of inter-sentence contextual information.}
elements.sent-2411-1010: Figure \ref{fig:shap illustrates the Shapley value decomposition, revealing a clear hierarchy in topic importance.}
elements.sent-2411-1011: Income Statement Analyses and Financial Ratio Analyses emerge as the most valuable components, contributing 67\% and 45\% of text-based $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS }$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-1012: Investment Thesis and Valuation form a second tier of importance, with Shapley values of 1.76\% and 1.51\%.
elements.sent-2411-1013: The remaining topics show minimal or negative Shapley values, indicating limited market reactions.
elements.sent-2411-1014: The findings' robustness is supported by several additional analyses.
elements.sent-2411-1015: Figure \ref{fig: shap_scale shows that normalizing Shapley values by sentence count and token length maintains Income Statement Analyses' dominance.}
elements.sent-2411-1016: Figure \ref{fig: shap_ind demonstrates consistency in topic rankings across different industries.}
elements.sent-2411-1017: The evolution of text-based $R^2_{\mathrm{OOS }$ is depicted in Figure \ref{fig:shap_yearly of the Online Appendix.}}

elements.sent-2411-1138: This paper examines both the quantitative and qualitative content of analyst reports.
elements.sent-2411-1139: I find that report text contains more market-valued information content than quantifiable forecast revisions economically, especially shortly following earnings announcements.
